name: Yoo et al. DREsS Synthetic Training Boundary Extract
population_or_context: Rubric-based automated essay scoring for English as a foreign language writing.
sample_size: DREsS includes 2,279 real classroom essays, 6,515 standardized existing-dataset samples, and 40,185 synthetic CASE corrupted essays.
methods: Extracted dataset composition, synthetic CASE generation, train/validation/test partitioning, and AES evaluation setup.
education_context: Undergraduate EFL argumentative writing with rubric dimensions for content, organization, and language.
response_origin: Real classroom essays and standardized existing essays are combined with DREsS_CASE synthetic corrupted essays; DREsS_Std and DREsS_CASE are used as training data only, while DREsS_New is split into train, validation, and test sets.
rubric_label_space: Rubric-based content, organization, and language scores for EFL writing.
label_source: Real and standardized essays use human/existing dataset labels; synthetic DREsS_CASE labels are generated through corruption aligned with target score ranges.
generation_model: CASE corruption strategy rather than an LLM generator.
generation_method: Synthetic essays are produced by corrupting essays to create lower-quality samples aligned with score ranges.
prompt_strategy: No LLM prompt strategy; generation uses the CASE corruption method.
evaluation_method: Automated essay scoring baselines are evaluated with rubric-score agreement, while real DREsS_New data is reserved for validation and test.
scorer_use: Training and data augmentation for rubric-based AES; not direct validation of a scorer using synthetic responses as the validation set.
real_response_comparator: DREsS_New real classroom essays provide the real-response train, validation, and test split, creating an explicit comparator boundary against synthetic CASE training augmentation.
reported_metrics: Paper reports 40,185 DREsS_CASE synthetic samples and rubric-based AES improvements; weighted kappa is used for scoring evaluation.
limitations: The synthetic subset is used for training, not as evidence that synthetic essays validate scorer accuracy.
The source is EFL essay scoring and may not generalize to short-answer, science-explanation, or SJT response scoring.
CASE synthetic generation differs from LLM-generated student response workflows.
